examples.title: Shannon Entropy Calculation
examples.description: Calculate Shannon entropy for a discrete probability distribution - fundamental measure of uncertainty in information theory
examples.code:
import numpy as np
import math

def calculate_entropy(probabilities):
    """
    Calculate Shannon entropy H(X) = -Σ p(x) * log2(p(x))
    
    Args:
        probabilities: Array of probabilities summing to 1
    
    Returns:
        Entropy value in bits
    """
    entropy = 0
    for p in probabilities:
        if p > 0:  # Avoid log(0)
            entropy -= p * math.log2(p)
    return entropy

# Example 1: Fair coin (maximum uncertainty)
coin_probs = [0.5, 0.5]
coin_entropy = calculate_entropy(coin_probs)
print(f"Fair Coin Entropy: {coin_entropy:.4f} bits")

# Example 2: Biased coin (less uncertainty)
biased_probs = [0.9, 0.1]
biased_entropy = calculate_entropy(biased_probs)
print(f"Biased Coin Entropy: {biased_entropy:.4f} bits")

# Example 3: Loaded die (6 outcomes)
die_probs = [1/6] * 6  # Fair die
die_entropy = calculate_entropy(die_probs)
print(f"Fair Die Entropy: {die_entropy:.4f} bits")

# Example 4: Non-uniform die
non_uniform_probs = [0.5, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1, 0.05, 0.05]
non_uniform_entropy = calculate_entropy(non_uniform_probs)
print(f"Non-uniform Die Entropy: {non_uniform_entropy:.4f} bits")

# Using numpy for vectorized calculation
def calculate_entropy_numpy(probabilities):
    p = np.array(probabilities)
    p = p[p > 0]  # Filter out zeros
    return -np.sum(p * np.log2(p))

print(f"\nUsing NumPy: {calculate_entropy_numpy(die_probs):.4f} bits")
examples.explanation: Shannon entropy measures the average amount of information/uncertainty. For a fair coin (2 outcomes, equal probability), entropy is maximum at 1 bit. A biased coin has lower entropy because outcomes are more predictable. The fair die with 6 equally likely outcomes has entropy log2(6) ≈ 2.58 bits.

examples.title: Mutual Information Calculation
examples.description: Calculate Mutual Information I(X;Y) - measures the dependence between two random variables, used in feature selection and classification
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter

def calculate_entropy(probabilities):
    """Calculate Shannon entropy"""
    return -np.sum([p * np.log2(p) for p in probabilities if p > 0])

def calculate_joint_entropy(joint_probs):
    """Calculate joint entropy H(X,Y)"""
    flat_probs = joint_probs.flatten()
    flat_probs = flat_probs[flat_probs > 0]
    return -np.sum(flat_probs * np.log2(flat_probs))

def calculate_mutual_information(pxy, px, py):
    """
    Calculate Mutual Information I(X;Y)
    I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)
    
    Args:
        pxy: Joint probability distribution P(X,Y)
        px: Marginal P(X)
        py: Marginal P(Y)
    """
    h_x = calculate_entropy(px)
    h_y = calculate_entropy(py)
    h_xy = calculate_joint_entropy(pxy)
    
    return h_x + h_y - h_xy

# Example: Weather vs Umbrella usage
# X = Weather (0:Sunny, 1:Rainy)
# Y = Umbrella (0:No, 1:Yes)

# Joint probability distribution
# P(X=0,Y=0) = 0.3, P(X=0,Y=1) = 0.1
# P(X=1,Y=0) = 0.1, P(X=1,Y=1) = 0.5
joint_probs = np.array([[0.3, 0.1], [0.1, 0.5]])

# Calculate marginal probabilities
px = joint_probs.sum(axis=1)  # [0.4, 0.6]
py = joint_probs.sum(axis=0)  # [0.4, 0.6]

# Calculate entropies
h_weather = calculate_entropy(px)
print(f"H(Weather): {h_weather:.4f} bits")

h_umbrella = calculate_entropy(py)
print(f"H(Umbrella): {h_umbrella:.4f} bits")

h_joint = calculate_joint_entropy(joint_probs)
print(f"H(Weather, Umbrella): {h_joint:.4f} bits")

mi = calculate_mutual_information(joint_probs, px, py)
print(f"Mutual Information I(Weather;Umbrella): {mi:.4f} bits")

# Using sklearn for verification
from sklearn.metrics import mutual_info_score

# Convert to categorical labels for sklearn
labels_weather = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]  # 0=Sunny, 1=Rainy
labels_umbrella = [0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1]  # 0=No, 1=Yes
print(f"\nSklearn MI Score: {mutual_info_score(labels_weather, labels_umbrella):.4f}")
examples.explanation: Mutual Information measures how much knowing one variable reduces uncertainty about another. Here we calculate I(Weather;Umbrella) which shows high dependency - when it rains, people use umbrellas. The formula I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) quantifies the shared information between variables.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n*m) where n,m are the number of unique values

examples.title: KL Divergence & Information Gain in Text Classification
examples.description: Apply KL divergence to compare word distributions between document classes - used in spam detection, sentiment analysis, and topic modeling
examples.code:
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
import math

def calculate_kl_divergence(p, q):
    """
    Calculate KL(P || Q) = Σ P(x) * log(P(x) / Q(x))
    
    Args:
        p: True distribution P (observed)
        q: Reference distribution Q (expected)
    
    Returns:
        KL divergence value
    """
    kl_div = 0
    for i in range(len(p)):
        if p[i] > 0 and q[i] > 0:
            kl_div += p[i] * math.log2(p[i] / q[i])
    return kl_div

def normalize_distribution(counts):
    """Convert counts to probability distribution"""
    total = sum(counts)
    return [c / total for c in counts]

# Example: Spam vs Non-Spam Email Classification
# Word frequency counts
spam_words = {'buy': 50, 'offer': 40, 'free': 35, 'win': 25, 'click': 20}
ham_words = {'meeting': 30, 'project': 25, 'report': 20, 'please': 15, 'thanks': 10}

# Get all unique words
all_words = list(set(spam_words.keys()) | set(ham_words.keys()))
print(f"Vocabulary: {all_words}\n")

# Create distributions
spam_counts = [spam_words.get(w, 0) for w in all_words]
ham_counts = [ham_words.get(w, 0) for w in all_words]

spam_dist = normalize_distribution(spam_counts)
ham_dist = normalize_distribution(ham_counts)

print("Spam Distribution:", {w: f"{p:.3f}" for w, p in zip(all_words, spam_dist)})
print("Ham Distribution:", {w: f"{3f}" for w, p in zip(all_words, ham_dist)})

# Calculate KL divergence: Spam || Ham
kl_spam_ham = calculate_kl_divergence(spam_dist, ham_dist)
print(f"\nKL(Spam || Ham): {kl_spam_ham:.4f} bits")

# Calculate KL divergence: Ham || Spam
kl_ham_spam = calculate_kl_divergence(ham_dist, spam_dist)
print(f"KL(Ham || Spam): {kl_ham_spam:.4f} bits")

# Information Gain calculation
def calculate_information_gain(px, pxy, py):
    """
    Calculate Information Gain: IG(Y|X) = H(Y) - H(Y|X)
    Also equals KL(P(Y|X) || P(Y))
    """
    h_y = -np.sum(py * np.log2(py + 1e-10))
    
    # H(Y|X) = Σ P(x) * H(Y|X=x)
    h_y_given_x = 0
    for i in range(len(px)):
        if px[i] > 0:
            py_given_x = pxy[i] / px[i] if px[i] > 0 else 0
            py_given_x = py_given_x[py_given_x > 0]
            h_y_given_x -= px[i] * np.sum(py_given_x * np.log2(py_given_x + 1e-10))
    
    return h_y - h_y_given_x

# Example: Feature selection using KL divergence
def word_discriminative_power(word, spam_dist, ham_dist):
    """
    Measure how discriminative a word is between classes
    using symmetric KL divergence (Jensen-Shannon)
    """
    # Get word probabilities
    p_spam = spam_dist[all_words.index(word)] if word in all_words else 0
    p_ham = ham_dist[all_words.index(word)] if word in all_words else 0
    
    # Average distribution
    m = [(p_spam + p_ham) / 2]
    
    if p_spam > 0 and m[0] > 0:
        kl1 = p_spam * math.log2(p_spam / m[0])
    else:
        kl1 = 0
    
    if p_ham > 0 and m[0] > 0:
        kl2 = p_ham * math.log2(p_ham / m[0])
    else:
        kl2 = 0
    
    return 0.5 * (kl1 + kl2)

print("\n--- Word Discriminative Power (Jensen-Shannon) ---")
for word in all_words:
    power = word_discriminative_power(word, spam_dist, ham_dist)
    print(f"'{word}': {power:.4f}")

print("\nMost discriminative words for spam detection!")
examples.explanation: KL divergence measures how different one distribution is from another. In spam detection, we compare word distributions in spam vs ham emails. Words like 'buy', 'offer', 'free' have high discriminative power (high KL divergence) making them good features for classification. The Jensen-Shannon divergence provides a symmetric measure useful for clustering and similarity.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for KL, O(v) for feature selection where v is vocabulary size
